Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I was going to study for a few hours each day, but it rarely worked out that way. I'd usually end up cramming when I felt the panic set in, which was most of the time. Being 27 and trying to juggle everything with limited resources made it hard to stick to any real schedule. I spent a lot of time just staring at materials feeling overwhelmed rather than actually making progress.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd have weeks where I barely touched anything, then suddenly I'd be up until 3am trying to catch up before a deadline. Other responsibilities kept pulling me away—work stuff, bills, life things that just don't stop because you're a student. The regularity I wanted never really materialized, and that inconsistency fed into my anxiety about the whole thing.
Q3: Usually fear, to be honest. I'd get that sinking feeling that I was falling behind and force myself to log in just to see how bad things had gotten. Sometimes the gamified task notifications would pull me in because they felt less intimidating than the actual coursework. Other times I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something, even if I wasn't really productive once I got there.
Q4: There was a brief period where logging in became somewhat routine, especially when I got into the gamified elements. Earning those small achievements gave me little dopamine hits that made me come back. But it didn't last—once the actual difficult content piled up and I started failing, the routine fell apart. The anxiety of seeing my poor progress made me avoid logging in altogether some days.
Q5: The videos were probably most useful for me since I'm a visual learner. I could pause them, rewatch sections, and it felt less overwhelming than reading dense text. The gamified tasks also helped because they broke things into smaller chunks that didn't feel as terrifying. When I could see progress bars filling up, it gave me some sense of accomplishment even when everything else felt like it was falling apart.
Q6: I barely touched the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. They just sat there making me feel guilty. The discussion forums also intimidated me—I'd post occasionally but reading through everyone else's intelligent contributions made me feel like I didn't belong. I kept telling myself I'd get to certain resources 'later' but later never came, and they just accumulated into this mountain of things I was avoiding.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could complete it and see immediate results. The gamified stuff worked because I got instant feedback and felt like I accomplished something. If something was going to take hours with no clear endpoint, I'd dread it and often put it off. I needed that sense of progress, something to show for my effort, otherwise my brain just couldn't justify the time investment.
Q8: Optional activities felt like traps, honestly. Like, they're supposed to help you, but when you're already drowning in required stuff, adding more feels impossible. I'd look at optional materials and think 'that would be nice' but then the required deadlines would loom and I'd abandon any optional plans. Required things had the weight of consequences behind them, which was sometimes the only thing that got me moving.
Q9: Yeah, I did a lot of studying that never showed up anywhere. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs, then study them on my phone during bus rides or at work during breaks. I couldn't always afford good internet at home, so offline studying was sometimes my only option. I'd also write notes by hand because it helped me remember things better than just clicking through screens.
Q10: There were whole weekends where I'd be reviewing printed materials or rewatching downloaded videos, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd sometimes screenshot important diagrams and study from my phone's gallery. When my internet was acting up, I'd work from whatever I'd managed to save previously. The system had no idea I was actually trying during those times.
Q11: Definitely. I'd access a video once, download it if I could, and then watch it multiple times offline. Same with lecture notes—I'd print them at the library when I had the chance and then study from paper for days. It was just more practical given my situation. The LMS probably thought I looked at something once and forgot about it, but that wasn't the reality.
Q12: There were several points where I just hit a wall. Around midterms, my anxiety spiked so badly I could barely function. The material kept getting harder, and I felt like I was falling further behind with no way to catch up. Financial stress was also eating at me—worrying about rent while trying to study for exams is a special kind of torture. Those periods, I'd log in and just freeze.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from working extra shifts, the last thing I could do was engage meaningfully with coursework. I'd log into the LMS out of guilt, click around aimlessly, maybe watch half a video before zoning out. The fatigue made everything harder to absorb. Competing responsibilities meant I was always stealing time from somewhere, and usually it was my studying that suffered.
Q14: More times than I want to admit. I'd have the LMS open while my mind was somewhere else entirely—worrying about money, about failing, about what I was going to do if this didn't work out. I'd technically be 'watching' a video but absorb nothing. It was like being physically present but mentally checked out. The anxiety made it impossible to focus even when I was trying.
Q15: All the time. The assignments especially—I'd know they were important, know the deadline was coming, but something in me would just freeze. It's like the bigger the task, the more scared I got, and fear made me avoid rather than engage. I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with the same dread. It's a terrible cycle that I couldn't break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me act. I'd be paralyzed until the deadline was breathing down my neck, then I'd panic-work through whatever I could manage. The LMS would probably show these spikes of activity right before due dates. It wasn't healthy or effective, but fear of the deadline was stronger than my fear of the material. Unfortunately, panic-work doesn't produce quality results.
Q17: Feedback was a mixed bag. When I got good feedback on my assignment scores, it motivated me briefly. But exam feedback just confirmed my fears that I wasn't getting it. Honestly, I started avoiding checking feedback because I was scared of what I'd see. The gamified tasks gave more positive reinforcement, which is probably why I gravitated toward those instead of facing the harder truths.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming to me. Ten online courses is a lot to juggle, and I never felt like I could give proper attention to any of them. The way things were laid out, I'd often lose track of what was due when across different courses. I needed more scaffolding, more hand-holding maybe, but the structure assumed a level of organization I couldn't maintain.
Q19: The gamified elements made engagement easier—they gave me small wins when everything else felt like losing. But the sheer volume of content made it harder. When I'd see how much material I'd missed or how far behind I was, it paralyzed me. Clearer priorities or a more guided path through the content would have helped me not feel so lost and scared all the time.
Q20: That's basically me. I was logging in constantly, clicking around, watching videos, doing the gamified stuff—but when it came to actually submitting real work, I choked. The anxiety of putting something out there to be judged was too much. It's easier to consume content than to produce something that proves whether you actually understand it. Fear of failure kept me busy but unproductive.
Q21: I can see how that happens. Some people are just more efficient, or they study in ways that don't show up online. Maybe they have better support systems or fewer distractions. For me, it was the opposite—lots of activity but I was spinning my wheels. I wish I could have been one of those people who could study smart instead of just study scared.
Q22: The log can show when you're present and what you clicked on, but it can't show whether anything actually went into your brain. It misses the offline studying, the anxiety that makes you stare blankly at a screen, the life circumstances that affect everything. It sees quantity of interaction but not quality of learning. It definitely can't measure how scared or overwhelmed someone feels.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with what you're learning, not just going through the motions. It means your brain is present, you're asking questions, you're building understanding. For me, I wanted to be engaged but my anxiety kept getting in the way. True engagement requires a mental state I struggled to achieve—being calm enough to actually learn instead of just survive.
Q24: The data wouldn't show how much I actually wanted to succeed, how many hours I spent worrying about failing, or how hard I tried in ways that didn't register digitally. It wouldn't show my offline studying, my handwritten notes, or the panic attacks before logging in. It wouldn't capture that I'm 27 trying to change my life with limited resources and high stakes. The human struggle behind the numbers is completely invisible.
